title: Fitting an in-line water filter under the sink
subtitle: Splice a single-stage cartridge filter into the cold water supply under the kitchen sink
excerpt: How to fit a canister-style in-line water filter to the cold water supply under a kitchen sink using compression fittings, covering how to cut the existing pipe, connect the filter housing, and restore supply without leaks.
sourceNotes: WRAS (Water Regulations Advisory Scheme) guidance on water fittings. CIBSE Guide G (public health engineering). Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics.

glossaryTerms.definition: A plumbing connector that seals by compressing a soft metal ring (the olive) against the pipe as the cap nut is tightened. No heat or solder is required; the joint is made entirely by spanner work. Suitable for copper and compatible plastic pipe. Overtightening distorts the olive and causes leaks; one-and-a-quarter turns past hand-tight is the standard tightening allowance.

content.text: An in-line filter connects into the cold water supply pipe using two

, one on each side of the filter housing, and a short length of 15 mm copper pipe. Most filter kits supply the fittings and a bracket to mount the canister under the sink. The only tool requirement beyond a spanner is the pipe cutter; the connection itself requires no specialist plumbing skill.

In-line filter kit (housing, cartridge, inlet and outlet compression fittings, mounting bracket). Short length of 15 mm copper pipe to bridge between the existing supply and the filter (measure the gap before buying). PTFE tape. Replacement cartridges for the filter type specified by the manufacturer.
Step 1, isolate the supply and drain down

Locate the service valve on the cold water supply pipe under the sink, most modern kitchens have one on a quarter-turn inline isolator. Turn it closed (slot across the pipe). Open the cold tap above to release pressure and drain the pipe. Hold a cloth under the pipe before cutting to catch residual water.
Step 2, cut the pipe and fit the filter

Fix the filter bracket to the inside of the sink cabinet. Measure the length of pipe to be removed, allowing for the length of the filter body plus the two short stub pipes needed to enter the compression fittings. Mark with the carpenters pencil. Cut the pipe with the pipe cutter: clamp the cutter on the line, tighten until it bites, and rotate around the pipe, tightening a quarter-turn every rotation. Deburr the cut ends with the reamer on the pipe cutter or with the sanding block. Remove a short section of pipe from between the cuts to create the gap for the filter.

Slide the cap nut and olive onto each cut pipe end before connecting to the filter. Push the pipe firmly into the filter fitting body until it seats against the stop. Tighten the cap nut by hand, then tighten with the adjustable spanner one-and-a-quarter turns further. Do not overtighten. Repeat on both sides of the filter. Check that the inlet and outlet ports are connected in the direction marked on the filter housing.
Step 4, restore supply and check for leaks

Open the isolation valve slowly. Allow water to flow through the filter for one minute to flush the new cartridge before using the water from the tap. Check all four compression joints for weeping while the system is under pressure. A slight dampness around a joint means the cap nut needs another quarter turn, no more. Dry the joint completely before re-checking to confirm the source.

attrs.symptom: Compression joint weeps after tightening to the specified amount
attrs.cause: The pipe end was not fully deburred, leaving a sharp edge that has cut into the olive; or the pipe was not pushed to the full depth of the fitting before the nut was tightened.
attrs.fix: Isolate and drain. Remove the fitting. Inspect the olive — if it is damaged, replace it. Reburr the pipe end and reassemble, ensuring the pipe seats fully in the fitting before tightening.

attrs.symptom: Water flow at the tap is reduced after fitting the filter
attrs.cause: The filter cartridge has a lower flow rate than the supply pipe, or the cartridge was fitted reversed.
attrs.fix: Check the flow arrow on the filter body and the cartridge. If the cartridge is reversed, drain and refit it in the correct orientation. If the flow rate is genuinely reduced by the cartridge specification, this is normal and will persist until the cartridge is replaced.
